I've tested this today with the latest RabbitMQ 3.5.3.
I've initialized a `Connection` with `heartbeat=60`. As a result:
1. When we negotiate connection parameters with RabbitMQ, we send this parameter. RabbitMQ then sends a heartbeat frame *every 60 seconds*.
2. Haigha timeouts after 60 seconds with no arriving bytes - which happens all the time. It does so silently, so nothing happens (which is a point I will address in future pull requests), but still - it's wrong. According to the spec, we should wait 120 seconds in this case.

We don't want to create a new logger here. Haigha has a system in place for configuring the logger for the connection and everything that it encompasses. The logger is either passed by the user to Connection or Connection will use its own default if one wasn't passed by the user. After that, Connection expects **that** logger to be used by the channels and transport. By creating a new logger here, we're interfering with what the user and/or Connection intended. See references to `self.connection.logger` in my example haigha PR https://github.com/agoragames/haigha/pull/80/files. You will also find references to `self.connection.logger` in `SocketTransport.read`, etc.

Actually, p2.x16 has a worse communication topology compared to p3.x16.
For p2.x16, the topology is very special. The communication net is composed of 2 fully connected subnets: the first 9 gpus and the last 7 gpus. Unlike p3.x16, there are no connections between the 2 components.
In that case, I expect that tree mode should still outperform device mode. However, surprisingly the device mode is better. Actually, the performance is similar to local mode on p2.x16, which seems weird to me.

It unfortunately does because ``max_in_memory_upload_chunks`` have a difference performance implication than ``max_in_memory_downloads``. 

Currently if I want to combine the two, the two would have to share the ``SlidingWindowSemaphore`` which may be slower than a regular semaphore because the release of the count of a semaphore is dependent on the lowest sequence number in-progress to finish and that may result in threads not being able to make requests until the lowest sequence number to finish.

With the regular semaphore, the sequence number has no affect on the count when the semaphore is released. So no matter what you can guarantee that if the semaphore is completely exhausted it is because there are ``max_in_memory_upload_chunk`` number of threads completing requests, which that guarantee cannot be made for streaming downloads. In the end, the sharing of the``SlidingWindowSemaphore`` could potentially slow down the in memory uploads.

I think I am on the stance that we re-address these parameters before ``s3transfer`` goes GA to see if we can combine the two (as I do think it is a little strange), but for now since it is not publicly exposed and it requires an additional feature to be able to support a ``max_in_memory_chunks`` parameter effectively.

I left the argument in `Get Matching Xpath Count` as _*xpath*_ but changed the argument in `Xpath Should Match X Times` to _*locator*_. Here I changed it so one wouldn't get an Robot Framework positional argument error. If, as in one of the acceptance tests, you wrote 

```RobotFramework
  Xpath Should Match X Times xpath=//input[@type="text"] ${1}
```

and the argument was _*xpath*_, Robot Framework would think you were placing a named/optional argument, _*xpath*_, before a positional argument of _*expected_xpath_count*_.

They don't have to be the same. For `Get Matching Xpath Count` since there is only one argument, which I thought _*xpath*_ was more descriptive, I left it. To avoid the unintentional error I described above I changed the argument to _*locator*_ under `Xpath Should Match X Times`.

Not a fan of this idea, as I think it makes it more tedious for the end user and makes the final code and interface more cluttered. Parameters like float comparison tolerance have traditionally been part of `assertEqual...` statements (e.g., Python standard lib, NumPy testing) and it's counter-intuitive / uncommon to have them set as class attributes for which you need to call an extra function to set. Similar for the other parameters - I feel they belong to `assertEqual`.

Subsequently, the implementation of (some of) these classes and/or their asserters depend on these parameters and might only be used within the scope of one call of this function. I like the re/un-registering pattern because
- it adds negligible overhead
- it's simple to understand and a simple invariant to maintain
- it cleans up for the next equality assertion no matter what problems come up and no matter how the user wants to mix our `assertEqual` variation with the vanilla one

@mfschwartz Can you take a peek at this change? It is different in two ways from your original change:

- It is in `download_to_filename` (vs. `download_to_file`). This method is the only method where we **know** we have created the file, so it is safe to delete it. In `download_to_file`, the user could pass in a non-file but file-like object, so there would be nothing to delete. (It should really be named `download_to_stream`, but we are already 1.0 so yay!)
- I am just calling `raise` to re-raise the error. The previous approach `raise exceptions.from_http_status` didn't make much sense because the status code would be 200 or 206 (and I think it would just be a 200). We don't have any corresponding error class, so it'd just [be][1] a generic `GoogleAPICallError`

[1]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/5cc463f934f165fc8685725d9f0b60a1043dd50d/core/google/api/core/exceptions.py#L337

From the docs 
> By default, each worker will have its PyTorch seed set to base_seed + worker_id, where base_seed is a long generated by main process using its RNG. However, seeds for other libraies may be duplicated upon initializing workers (w.g., NumPy), causing each worker to return identical random numbers. (See My data loader workers return identical random numbers section in FAQ.) You may use torch.initial_seed() to access the PyTorch seed for each worker in worker_init_fn, and use it to set other seeds before data loading.

So isn't it better to use 

```python
def worker_init_fn(worker):
    np.random.seed(torch.initial_seed())
```

@mitar How do you handle the conflict between `isort` and `yapf`? `yapf` does not hold a fix style, take the diff output of `yapf` for example:
```
-from ray.ray_constants import (AUTOSCALER_HEARTBEAT_TIMEOUT_S,
-                               AUTOSCALER_MAX_CONCURRENT_LAUNCHES,
-                               AUTOSCALER_MAX_LAUNCH_BATCH,
-                               AUTOSCALER_MAX_NUM_FAILURES,
-                               AUTOSCALER_UPDATE_INTERVAL_S)
+from ray.ray_constants import (
+    AUTOSCALER_HEARTBEAT_TIMEOUT_S, AUTOSCALER_MAX_CONCURRENT_LAUNCHES,
+    AUTOSCALER_MAX_LAUNCH_BATCH, AUTOSCALER_MAX_NUM_FAILURES,
+    AUTOSCALER_UPDATE_INTERVAL_S)
```
and 
```
-from ray.autoscaler.tags import (TAG_RAY_LAUNCH_CONFIG,
-                                 TAG_RAY_NODE_NAME, TAG_RAY_NODE_TYPE)
+from ray.autoscaler.tags import (TAG_RAY_LAUNCH_CONFIG, TAG_RAY_NODE_NAME,
+                                 TAG_RAY_NODE_TYPE)
```
Another thing difference is that `yapf` won't allow empty line between imports and file comments `'''file comments'''`, which `isort` should level 1 empty line. If we set `lines_after_imports=1`, it breaks the rule that there should be 2 empty lines before class or function.
